title: DisjointHardInterventions

tex_statement.html: Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CADMG and $W_1 \ins J \cup V$ and $W_2 \ins V$ two disjoint subsets of nodes from $G$. Then the CADMG obtained from first hard intervening on $W_1$ and then node-splitting on $W_2$ is the same CADMG that arises from first node-splitting on $W_2$ and then hard intervening on $W_1$. \[ \lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)} = \lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}. \]
tex_statement.env_title: Disjoint hard interventions and node-splitting hard interventions commute

tex_proof.raw:
% Claim proof file for row claim_3_11 -- "DisjointHardInterventions".
%
% Proof lifted from lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex:673--699 (the
% \Claude{...} block immediately following the claim). Only light edits:
% explicit \refrow{def_3_10} and \refrow{def_3_12} cross-links the first
% time \doit and \swig appear in the body, \medskip\noindent\emph{...}
% section headers mirroring the bare-NS sibling
% claim_3_8_proof_DisjointHardInterventions.tex, a \checkmark at the end
% of each component-agree paragraph, expansion of the disjoint-copy case
% analysis to match the sibling's level of detail, a one-phrase inline
% note recording the SWIG-vs-NS edge-set difference (the SWIG's inner
% hard intervention erases the bare-split edges), and a closing
% one-liner. The \Claude{...} wrapper from the LN source is dropped.
%
% Compiles standalone via the `subfiles` package.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: claim_3_11 (proof, with statement restated for readability)
% title: DisjointHardInterventions
\def\rowref{claim\_3\_11}
\phantomsection\label{claim_3_11}
%
% Cross-references: see claim_<ref>_statement_<title>.tex in this folder
% for the corresponding statement.

% --- statement (restated) ---------------------------------------------------
\begin{Lem}[Disjoint hard interventions and node-splitting hard interventions commute]
   Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CADMG and $W_1 \ins J \cup V$ and $W_2 \ins V$ two disjoint subsets of nodes from $G$.
   Then the CADMG obtained from first hard intervening on $W_1$ and then node-splitting on $W_2$ is the same CADMG that arises from first node-splitting on $W_2$ and then hard intervening on $W_1$.
      \[ \lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)} =  \lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}.   \]
\end{Lem}

% --- proof ------------------------------------------------------------------
\begin{proof}
\medskip\noindent\emph{Preamble.}
Since $W_1 \ins J \cup V$ and $W_2 \ins V$ are disjoint, both operations are
well-defined in either order: on the left, $\swig(W_2)$ acts on $G_{\doit(W_1)}$
whose output set $V \sm W_1$ still contains $W_2$ (using $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$);
on the right, $\doit(W_1)$ acts on $G_{\swig(W_2)}$, whose input/output universe
contains every vertex of $W_1 \ins J \cup V$ untouched by the split (again using
$W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$, so $W_1 \ins J \cup (V \sm W_2)$). For $w \in W_2$ we
write $w^o$ (output copy) and $w^i$ (input copy) for the two SWIG copies of $w$;
for $v \notin W_2$ we identify $v^o = v^i = v$. Each side of the displayed equation
is a CADMG presented as a 4-tuple $(J_\ast, V_\ast, E_\ast, L_\ast)$, and we verify
the four components in turn.

\medskip\noindent\emph{Node sets.}
Left: by \refrow{def_3_10}, $G_{\doit(W_1)}$ has input set $J \cup W_1$ and output
set $V \sm W_1$; then $\swig(W_2)$~\refrow{def_3_12} (with $W_2 \ins V \sm W_1$,
by $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$) gives
\[
  (J \cup W_1) \dcup W_2^i
  \qquad\text{and}\qquad
  (V \sm W_1 \sm W_2) \dcup W_2^o.
\]
Right: $G_{\swig(W_2)}$ has input set $J \dcup W_2^i$ and output set
$(V \sm W_2) \dcup W_2^o$; then $\doit(W_1)$ (with $W_1 \cap W_2^o = \emptyset$,
since $W_2^o$ consists of fresh output copies of vertices in $W_2$ and
$W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$, so $W_1 \ins J \cup (V \sm W_2)$) gives
\[
  (J \cup W_1) \dcup W_2^i
  \qquad\text{and}\qquad
  (V \sm W_1 \sm W_2) \dcup W_2^o.
\]
These agree. \checkmark

\medskip\noindent\emph{Directed edges.}
Left: $E_{\doit(W_1)} = E \sm \lC v \tuh w \mid w \in W_1 \rC$; then $\swig(W_2)$
(whose inner hard intervention erases the bare-split edges $w^i \tuh w^o$ that
node-splitting alone would produce) yields
\[
  \lC v_1^i \tuh v_2^o \mid v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E,\ v_2 \notin W_1 \rC.
\]
Right: $E_{\swig(W_2)} = \lC v_1^i \tuh v_2^o \mid v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E \rC$;
then $\doit(W_1)$ removes the edges with head in $W_1$. An edge $v_1^i \tuh v_2^o$
has head $v_2^o \in W_1$ iff $v_2 \in W_1$ (using $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$: if
$v_2 \in W_2$ then $v_2^o$ is a fresh output copy not in $W_1$, and if $v_2 \notin W_2$
then $v_2^o = v_2$). After removal we obtain
\[
  \lC v_1^i \tuh v_2^o \mid v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E,\ v_2 \notin W_1 \rC,
\]
which agrees with the left-hand side. \checkmark

\medskip\noindent\emph{Bidirected edges.}
Left: $L_{\doit(W_1)} = L \sm \lC v \huh w \mid w \in W_1 \rC$; then $\swig(W_2)$
yields
\[
  \lC v_1^o \huh v_2^o \mid v_1 \huh v_2 \in L,\ v_1, v_2 \notin W_1 \rC.
\]
Right: $L_{\swig(W_2)} = \lC v_1^o \huh v_2^o \mid v_1 \huh v_2 \in L \rC$;
then $\doit(W_1)$ removes the bidirected edges touching $W_1$. Using
$v_k^o \in W_1 \iff v_k \in W_1$ for $k = 1, 2$ (same fresh-copy / identity case
analysis as for the directed edges, again using $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$), the
result is
\[
  \lC v_1^o \huh v_2^o \mid v_1 \huh v_2 \in L,\ v_1, v_2 \notin W_1 \rC,
\]
which agrees with the left-hand side. \checkmark

All four components agree, completing the proof.
\end{proof}

\end{document}
tex_proof.html:
Lemma — Disjoint hard interventions and node-splitting hard interventions commute

Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CADMG and $W_1 \ins J \cup V$ and $W_2 \ins V$ two disjoint subsets of nodes from $G$. Then the CADMG obtained from first hard intervening on $W_1$ and then node-splitting on $W_2$ is the same CADMG that arises from first node-splitting on $W_2$ and then hard intervening on $W_1$. \[ \lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)} = \lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}. \]

Proof.

Preamble. Since $W_1 \ins J \cup V$ and $W_2 \ins V$ are disjoint, both operations are well-defined in either order: on the left, $\swig(W_2)$ acts on $G_{\doit(W_1)}$ whose output set $V \sm W_1$ still contains $W_2$ (using $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$); on the right, $\doit(W_1)$ acts on $G_{\swig(W_2)}$, whose input/output universe contains every vertex of $W_1 \ins J \cup V$ untouched by the split (again using $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$, so $W_1 \ins J \cup (V \sm W_2)$). For $w \in W_2$ we write $w^o$ (output copy) and $w^i$ (input copy) for the two SWIG copies of $w$; for $v \notin W_2$ we identify $v^o = v^i = v$. Each side of the displayed equation is a CADMG presented as a 4-tuple $(J_\ast, V_\ast, E_\ast, L_\ast)$, and we verify the four components in turn.

Node sets. Left: by def_3_10, $G_{\doit(W_1)}$ has input set $J \cup W_1$ and output set $V \sm W_1$; then $\swig(W_2)$ def_3_12 (with $W_2 \ins V \sm W_1$, by $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$) gives \[ (J \cup W_1) \dcup W_2^i \qquad\text{and}\qquad (V \sm W_1 \sm W_2) \dcup W_2^o. \] Right: $G_{\swig(W_2)}$ has input set $J \dcup W_2^i$ and output set $(V \sm W_2) \dcup W_2^o$; then $\doit(W_1)$ (with $W_1 \cap W_2^o = \emptyset$, since $W_2^o$ consists of fresh output copies of vertices in $W_2$ and $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$, so $W_1 \ins J \cup (V \sm W_2)$) gives \[ (J \cup W_1) \dcup W_2^i \qquad\text{and}\qquad (V \sm W_1 \sm W_2) \dcup W_2^o. \] These agree. \checkmark

Directed edges. Left: $E_{\doit(W_1)} = E \sm \lC v \tuh w \mid w \in W_1 \rC$; then $\swig(W_2)$ (whose inner hard intervention erases the bare-split edges $w^i \tuh w^o$ that node-splitting alone would produce) yields \[ \lC v_1^i \tuh v_2^o \mid v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E,\ v_2 \notin W_1 \rC. \] Right: $E_{\swig(W_2)} = \lC v_1^i \tuh v_2^o \mid v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E \rC$; then $\doit(W_1)$ removes the edges with head in $W_1$. An edge $v_1^i \tuh v_2^o$ has head $v_2^o \in W_1$ iff $v_2 \in W_1$ (using $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$: if $v_2 \in W_2$ then $v_2^o$ is a fresh output copy not in $W_1$, and if $v_2 \notin W_2$ then $v_2^o = v_2$). After removal we obtain \[ \lC v_1^i \tuh v_2^o \mid v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E,\ v_2 \notin W_1 \rC, \] which agrees with the left-hand side. \checkmark

Bidirected edges. Left: $L_{\doit(W_1)} = L \sm \lC v \huh w \mid w \in W_1 \rC$; then $\swig(W_2)$ yields \[ \lC v_1^o \huh v_2^o \mid v_1 \huh v_2 \in L,\ v_1, v_2 \notin W_1 \rC. \] Right: $L_{\swig(W_2)} = \lC v_1^o \huh v_2^o \mid v_1 \huh v_2 \in L \rC$; then $\doit(W_1)$ removes the bidirected edges touching $W_1$. Using $v_k^o \in W_1 \iff v_k \in W_1$ for $k = 1, 2$ (same fresh-copy / identity case analysis as for the directed edges, again using $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$), the result is \[ \lC v_1^o \huh v_2^o \mid v_1 \huh v_2 \in L,\ v_1, v_2 \notin W_1 \rC, \] which agrees with the left-hand side. \checkmark

All four components agree, completing the proof.

lean.name: hardInterventionOn_swig_comm
lean.code:
/-- claim_3_11 (`DisjointHardInterventions`): for a CDMG `G : CDMG α`
and disjoint subsets `W₁ W₂ : Set α` with `W₂ ⊆ G.V`, hard intervention
on `W₁` and the node-splitting hard intervention (SWIG) on `W₂`
commute. Mirrors the displayed equation in the `\Lem` at
`lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` line 669. -/
theorem hardInterventionOn_swig_comm
    {G : CDMG α} {W₁ W₂ : Set α}
    (hW₂ : W₂ ⊆ G.V) (hdisj : Disjoint W₁ W₂) :
    (G.hardInterventionOn W₁).swig W₂
        (subset_hardInterventionOn_V_of_disjoint hW₂ hdisj)
      = (G.swig W₂ hW₂).hardInterventionOn (Sum.inl '' W₁) := by

lean_explanation:
The LN states an equality of CDMGs $(G_{\doit(W_1)})_{\swig(W_2)} = (G_{\swig(W_2)})_{\doit(W_1)}$ between two ways of composing a hard intervention and a node-splitting hard intervention (SWIG). The Lean encoding pins this as a literal `Eq` of CDMGs because both sides share the carrier `α ⊕ ↑W₂` — the carrier inherited from a single SWIG on $W_2$.

`α : Type*` is the ambient vertex type, matching the LN's silent assumption that $G$ lives over some unspecified vertex set; `G : CDMG α` is $G = (J, V, E, L)$; `W₁ W₂ : Set α` are the two subsets. The hypothesis `hW₂ : W₂ ⊆ G.V` is the LN's $W_2 \subseteq V$; `Disjoint W₁ W₂` is Mathlib's order-theoretic disjointness predicate, which on `Set` unfolds to $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$. The LN's third precondition $W_1 \subseteq J \cup V$ does not appear as a Lean hypothesis; see the design notes.

The discharge `subset_hardInterventionOn_V_of_disjoint hW₂ hdisj` packages the bridging inclusion $W_2 \subseteq V \setminus W_1$ that the LN states parenthetically. The helper itself lives in the bare-NS sibling claim_3_8's file (`HardInterventionNodeSplittingCommute.lean`), where it was introduced and explicitly kept public for this row's re-use; it discharges the precondition of the inner `swig W₂` call on the LHS, whose vertex set has already been reduced by the hard intervention to $V \setminus W_1$.

Mathlib idioms worth flagging: `Set.image` (written infix as `''`) lifts a function pointwise to sets, so `Sum.inl '' W₁ : Set (α ⊕ ↑W₂)` is the canonical embedding of $W_1$ into the post-SWIG carrier via the LN's "0-copy" identification of $\alpha$ with $\mathrm{inl}(\alpha)$. `↑W₂` is the coerced subtype `{x // x ∈ W₂}` — Lean's way of carrying the LN's output copies $w^o$ for $w \in W_2$. `Disjoint` comes from Mathlib's lattice API rather than a primitive set predicate, which lets it compose uniformly with the rest of the order library. The surface name `G.swig W hW` is a `noncomputable abbrev` for the long-form `nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn` (def_3_12), chosen here so the Lean statement reads as close to the LN's $G_{\swig(W)}$ notation as possible.

The theorem takes the implicit data `G W₁ W₂` and the two explicit hypotheses `hW₂` and `hdisj`, and concludes literal `Eq` of two CDMGs over `α ⊕ ↑W₂`. The LHS applies `hardInterventionOn` first (carrier-preserving on `α`) and `swig` second (extending to `α ⊕ ↑W₂`); the RHS applies them in the opposite order, with the LN's "the same $W_1$" on the outer HI spelled out as the lift `Sum.inl '' W₁` because the outer HI now lives over the post-SWIG carrier.
design_choices:
**Literal `Eq`, not `CDMGEquiv`.** Both sides have carrier `α ⊕ ↑W₂`, definitionally equal across the two compositions: on the LHS `hardInterventionOn W₁` is carrier-preserving (`α → α`) and `swig W₂` then extends to `α ⊕ ↑W₂`; on the RHS `swig W₂` extends first to `α ⊕ ↑W₂` and `hardInterventionOn (Sum.inl '' W₁)` then preserves that carrier. The SWIG itself inherits its carrier shape verbatim from `nodeSplittingOn` — the inner hard-intervention layer of `swig` does not touch the carrier, only deleting the `range Sum.inr` summand of the vertex set — so the two iterated carriers match the bare-NS sibling claim_3_8 exactly. No re-labeling bijection is needed and literal `Eq` of CDMGs is type-correct. This puts the row in the same regime as claim_3_4 and claim_3_8 (literal-`Eq` family). The contrast is the SWIG--SWIG sibling claim_3_10, which iterates *two* SWIGs on disjoint sets: there the carriers are `(α ⊕ ↑W₁) ⊕ ↑(Sum.inl '' W₂)` versus `(α ⊕ ↑W₂) ⊕ ↑(Sum.inl '' W₁)`, definitionally distinct, forcing `CDMGEquiv`. Here `swig` is applied only once and on the *same* set on each side, and the partner operation is the carrier-preserving `hardInterventionOn`; that absorbs the iteration without forcing a re-labeling bijection.

**No `W₁ ⊆ J ∪ V` precondition.** The LN writes $W_1 \subseteq J \cup V$ as part of its preamble, but `hardInterventionOn` is well-defined for every `W₁ : Set α`, and vertices in $W_1 \setminus (J \cup V)$ are inert: no edge has an endpoint there (by `E_subset` / `L_subset`), so removing them under hard intervention is the identity on all four CDMG data fields. They ride along the LHS = RHS equality without contributing anything, and the commute identity holds for arbitrary `W₁`. The LN's $W_1 \subseteq J \cup V$ is informal scaffolding to make $\doit(W_1)$ mathematically meaningful in prose — dropping it makes the lemma maximally useful at call sites without weakening it. This is the same drop made in the bare-NS sibling claim_3_8.

**Reuse the bridging helper from claim_3_8 rather than redeclaring it.** The LHS's inner `swig W₂` requires $W_2 \subseteq (G.\mathrm{hardInterventionOn}\,W_1).V = V \setminus W_1$, which follows from `hW₂` together with disjointness. That small bridge is `subset_hardInterventionOn_V_of_disjoint`, introduced for claim_3_8 and explicitly kept public there with this row in mind as the anticipated second consumer. We use it verbatim. Both `hW₂` and `hdisj` are therefore load-bearing for typing alone: dropping either makes the LHS fail to elaborate.

**Lift `W₁ ↦ Sum.inl '' W₁` on the RHS's HI argument.** The outer operation on the RHS is hard intervention on the post-SWIG graph, which lives over the carrier `α ⊕ ↑W₂`. Its target set must therefore be a `Set (α ⊕ ↑W₂)`, not a `Set α`. Under the convention `Sum.inl = 0-copy = canonical observation copy` from def_3_11 / def_3_12, the LN's "the same $W_1$" *is* the `inl`-image of $W_1$ in the SWIG carrier; the LN identifies $\alpha \cong \mathrm{inl}(\alpha)$ implicitly throughout ("we make the identification $W = W^o$" at `graphs.tex` line 197), but Lean's stricter type discipline forces us to spell the lift out. For SWIG specifically, $V$ is just $\mathrm{Sum.inl}\,\!''\,G.V$ — the `range Sum.inr` piece that bare `nodeSplittingOn` would carry is killed by the SWIG's inner hard intervention — so removing `Sum.inl '' W₁` from the post-SWIG graph mirrors "then $\doit(W_1)$" on the right-hand side exactly. The narrower lift `Sum.inl '' (W₁ ∩ (G.J ∪ G.V))` was rejected on the same two counts as in claim_3_8: by inertness it would yield the same RHS CDMG, and it would force every call site to thread $W_1 \cap (G.J \cup G.V)$ as an extra ingredient.

**Surface API `G.swig W hW`, not the long-form `nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn`.** The four `@[simp]` characterisation lemmas for the SWIG (`nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn_J`, `_V`, `mem_..._E`, `mem_..._L`) are stated about the long-form name but fire on `G.swig W hW` by reducibility, because `swig` is declared as a `noncomputable abbrev`. Choosing `swig` in the statement therefore loses nothing simp-wise and gains the LN's notation verbatim (the prose $G_{\swig(W)}$ reads as `G.swig W` in Lean).

**Argument order `hW₂` before `hdisj`.** Mirrors the signature of the bare-NS sibling `hardInterventionOn_nodeSplittingOn_comm` (claim_3_8). The two `_comm` lemmas form a parallel pair, and identical hypothesis order lets a downstream consumer swap one for the other with zero call-site churn. It also matches the helper `subset_hardInterventionOn_V_of_disjoint`'s own argument order, so the bridging discharge slots in left-to-right without re-ordering.

**Naming `hardInterventionOn_swig_comm`.** Follows the Mathlib `_comm` convention for commutativity of two operators (`add_comm`, `mul_comm`, `Set.union_comm`), with both operators in the name and ordered left-to-right to match the LHS of the conclusion. The subsection's commute-lemma family now reads as a uniform menu: `_comm` for the literal-`Eq` rows (`hardInterventionOn_comm` for claim_3_4, `hardInterventionOn_nodeSplittingOn_comm` for claim_3_8, `hardInterventionOn_swig_comm` here) and `_comm_equiv` for the `CDMGEquiv` rows (`nodeSplittingOn_comm_equiv` for claim_3_7, `swig_comm_equiv` for claim_3_10). The mirror `swig_hardInterventionOn_comm` (operators reversed) is technically equivalent but not exposed — consumers can use `.symm` if they want the swap.

**Distinct from the downstream claim_3_15.** A future reader should not collapse this row with claim_3_15 ("Adding intervention nodes commutes with disjoint node-splitting hard interventions"), which states a *different* identity $(G_{\swig(W_1)})_{\doit(I_{W_2})} = (G_{\doit(I_{W_2})})_{\swig(W_1)}$, where $\doit(I_{W_2})$ is the intervention on the *intervention-node extension* of $W_2$ rather than on $W_2$ itself. claim_3_11 is the direct SWIG-mirror of claim_3_8 — foundational, not a special case or corollary of any later commute lemma — and the two displayed equations only look superficially similar.
